---
title: "Model Predictive Control and Constraint Handling"
description: "How MPC solves a finite-horizon control problem repeatedly, why receding-horizon replanning handles constraints naturally, and how feasibility and stability depend on the optimization design."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: control-and-dynamics
prerequisites:
  - /topics/advanced/control-and-dynamics/concepts/linear-quadratic-regulation-and-riccati-intuition.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/numerical-methods/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain why MPC repeatedly solves a finite-horizon optimization problem online."
  - "Describe receding-horizon control and why only the first control action is applied."
  - "Recognize why MPC handles state and input constraints naturally."
  - "State the first-pass role of feasibility, terminal ingredients, and stability guarantees."
applications:
  - control-and-dynamics
  - engineering-systems
  - scientific-computing
keywords:
  - MPC
  - model predictive control
  - receding horizon
  - constraints
  - optimal control
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/advanced/control-and-dynamics/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/control-and-dynamics/concepts/linear-quadratic-regulation-and-riccati-intuition.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/control-and-dynamics/concepts/estimation-kalman-filtering-and-the-separation-principle.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/numerical-methods/index.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "MIT 16.323 Principles of Optimal Control"
  - "Stanford EE364b Convex Optimization II"
  - "Stanford AA203 Optimal and Learning-Based Control"
current-sources:
  - "MIT 16.323 model predictive control"
  - "MIT 16.323 lecture notes"
  - "Stanford EE364b course information"
  - "Stanford EE364b lecture slides and notes"
  - "Stanford EE364b model predictive control slides"
  - "Stanford AA203 bulletin"
---

## Role

This is the sixth page of the `Control and Dynamics` module.

Its job is to explain what happens when we want optimal control with explicit constraints:

`we solve a finite-horizon control problem online, apply only the first action, then re-solve at the next step`

That is the first-pass entry point into model predictive control.

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after [Estimation, Kalman Filtering, and the Separation Principle](estimation-kalman-filtering-and-the-separation-principle.qmd).

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- why MPC is a repeated finite-horizon optimization method
- what receding horizon means
- why constraints are the main reason MPC is so useful
- why feasibility and stability are design questions, not automatic consequences

## Why It Matters

LQR is elegant, but it does not directly handle many practical requirements such as:

- actuator saturation
- safety bounds on state variables
- input-rate limits
- hard operational constraints over time

MPC addresses this by putting the control problem into an optimization loop that explicitly includes those constraints.

At each time step, it:

- predicts future trajectories over a finite horizon
- optimizes a control sequence
- applies only the first control action
- repeats after receiving the new state estimate

So this page is where the site turns:

`optimal control law from one derivation`

into

`online constrained optimization running inside the controller`

## Prerequisite Recall

- LQR solved a structured unconstrained quadratic optimal-control problem
- Kalman filtering gave a recursive state estimate for noisy systems
- optimization under constraints needs explicit feasible sets and objective design
- numerical methods matter because MPC is only useful if the optimization can be solved fast enough

## Intuition

### MPC Plans Ahead, But Does Not Commit Forever

MPC solves a finite-horizon problem over future inputs

$$
u_0,u_1,\dots,u_{N-1},
$$

but it only applies `u_0`.

At the next step, it shifts the horizon forward and solves again.

This is the `receding-horizon` idea.

### Constraints Are First-Class Objects

In MPC, constraints are not an afterthought.

They are part of the optimization problem:

- `x_t \in X`
- `u_t \in U`

That is why MPC is often preferred when saturations, envelopes, or safety limits matter.

### Prediction Model Plus Online Optimization Gives Flexibility

MPC uses the model to forecast what future states would happen under candidate controls.

Then optimization chooses the best feasible plan under the cost and constraints.

So MPC lives exactly at the intersection of:

- dynamics
- optimization
- computation

### Stability Is Not Free

Because MPC repeatedly solves truncated finite-horizon problems, stability is not automatic.

Terminal costs, terminal constraints, and feasible-set design are often what make the closed-loop behavior well-behaved.

## Formal Core

::: {#def-finite-horizon-ocp}
## Definition: Finite-Horizon Optimal Control Problem

At a first pass, the discrete-time constrained control problem is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & \sum_{t=0}^{N-1}\ell(x_t,u_t) + \ell_f(x_N) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & x_{t+1}=f(x_t,u_t), \\
& x_t \in X,\quad u_t \in U, \\
& x_0 \text{ given}.
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

::: {#def-mpc}
## Definition: Model Predictive Control

Model predictive control solves a finite-horizon optimal control problem at each time step, applies only the first control action, then repeats the optimization at the next step using updated state information.
:::

::: {#def-receding-horizon}
## Definition: Receding Horizon Policy

If the optimizer returns the sequence

$$
u_0^\star,u_1^\star,\dots,u_{N-1}^\star,
$$

MPC applies only `u_0^\star`, advances the system one step, and re-solves from the new state.
:::

::: {#def-feasibility}
## Definition: Recursive Feasibility

At a first pass, `recursive feasibility` means:

if the MPC problem is feasible at the current step and we apply the resulting first action, then the next-step MPC problem remains feasible as the system evolves.
:::

::: {#thm-terminal-ingredients}
## Theorem Idea: Terminal Ingredients Support Stability

Under standard assumptions, suitable terminal cost and terminal constraint choices can make the receding-horizon controller inherit a useful stability guarantee.
:::

At a first pass, this means the optimization problem is not only about cost. Its structure matters for long-time behavior.

## Worked Example

Consider the scalar system

$$
x_{k+1}=x_k+u_k
$$

with input constraint

$$
|u_k|\le 1
$$

and state constraint

$$
|x_k|\le 5.
$$

Suppose the cost over horizon `N=3` is

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{2}(x_t^2+0.1u_t^2) + x_3^2.
$$

If the current state is `x_0=4.5`, then the controller cannot simply choose an unconstrained aggressive move if that would violate the actuator limit.

Instead, MPC searches over the feasible control sequence and picks the best one that respects:

- the dynamics
- the input bound
- the state bound

This example shows why MPC is different from pure LQR intuition:

- the model predicts future consequences
- the optimizer respects explicit limits
- the resulting first action is chosen from a feasible sequence, not from an unconstrained closed-form formula alone

## Computation Lens

When you see an MPC setup, ask:

1. what prediction model is being used, and how accurate is it over the planning horizon?
2. what are the hard state and input constraints?
3. what horizon length is long enough to matter but short enough to solve online?
4. is the optimization problem convex, or is this a nonlinear or nonconvex MPC problem?
5. what terminal ingredients are being used to support recursive feasibility or stability?

These questions are usually more important than memorizing a generic block optimization form.

## Application Lens

### Real-Time Constrained Control

MPC is widely used when constraints are central, such as in energy systems, process control, autonomous systems, and trajectory planning.

### Optimization In The Loop

This is one of the clearest cases where optimization is not just an offline design tool but part of the controller itself.

### Bridge To Learning-Based Control

The next natural step is where model quality becomes part of the problem:

if the model is learned, adapted, or uncertain, then control starts blending into system identification and reinforcement learning.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you can now explain:

- why MPC repeatedly solves a finite-horizon problem instead of one infinite-horizon law once and for all
- what receding horizon means
- why constraints make MPC attractive
- why feasibility and stability need design attention
- why MPC sits at the intersection of dynamics, optimization, and computation

then this page has done its job.

## Go Deeper

The next natural step in this module is:

- [Learning-Based Control, System Identification, and RL Bridges](learning-based-control-system-identification-and-rl-bridges.qmd)

The strongest adjacent live pages right now are:

- [Control and Dynamics](../index.qmd)
- [Linear Quadratic Regulation and Riccati Intuition](linear-quadratic-regulation-and-riccati-intuition.qmd)
- [Estimation, Kalman Filtering, and the Separation Principle](estimation-kalman-filtering-and-the-separation-principle.qmd)
- [Learning-Based Control, System Identification, and RL Bridges](learning-based-control-system-identification-and-rl-bridges.qmd)
- [Optimization](../../optimization/index.qmd)
- [Numerical Methods](../../numerical-methods/index.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this page are:

- [MIT 16.323 Lecture 16: Model Predictive Control](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/16-323-principles-of-optimal-control-spring-2008/resources/lec16/) - official lecture notes page for receding-horizon control and constrained optimal-control structure. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 16.323 lecture notes](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/16-323-principles-of-optimal-control-spring-2008/pages/lecture-notes/) - official notes index for dynamic programming, MPC, and optimal-control context. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE364b course information](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364b/course.html) - official recent course page for convex optimization with control-facing applications. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE364b: Lecture Slides and Notes](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364b/lectures.html) - official lecture index including MPC and stochastic MPC materials. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE364b: Model Predictive Control slides](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364b/lectures/mpc_slides.pdf) - official Stanford slides for linear time-invariant convex optimal control and receding-horizon design. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford AA203 bulletin](https://22-23.bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2136621) - official archived course description that explicitly includes an introduction to model predictive control. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 16.323 Lecture 16: Model Predictive Control](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/16-323-principles-of-optimal-control-spring-2008/resources/lec16/) - `First pass` - official notes page for finite-horizon receding-horizon control. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 16.323 lecture notes](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/16-323-principles-of-optimal-control-spring-2008/pages/lecture-notes/) - `Second pass` - official notes index for the broader optimal-control context around MPC. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE364b course information](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364b/course.html) - `Second pass` - official recent course page for optimization methods that feed directly into control applications. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE364b: Lecture Slides and Notes](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364b/lectures.html) - `Second pass` - official lecture index showing where MPC fits inside convex optimization and control. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE364b: Model Predictive Control slides](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364b/lectures/mpc_slides.pdf) - `Second pass` - official slides for convex finite-horizon control and receding-horizon policy design. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford AA203 bulletin](https://22-23.bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2136621) - `Second pass` - official archived description connecting MPC to broader optimal and learning-based control. Checked `2026-04-25`.
